task_description:
# CSRF-Protected Comments API with RedwoodSDK

## Background
You are extending a freshly scaffolded RedwoodSDK (`rwsdk`) project. Build a small comments API protected by a real CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) defense using rwsdk's request pipeline (global middleware and/or per-route interrupters). The API serves a public read endpoint and a write endpoint that requires a per-session CSRF token, plus an unauthenticated health endpoint that is always reachable.

## Requirements
- The web app is a RedwoodSDK project served by `npm run dev` (Vite + Cloudflare workerd).
- Mount a CSRF guard in rwsdk's request pipeline so that for any non-safe HTTP method (POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) the request is rejected unless an `X-CSRF-Token` header (or a `_csrf` form field) matches a token stored in a `csrf` cookie on the same request.
- Provide a token issuance endpoint that mints fresh CSRF tokens, sets them in a cookie, and returns them in JSON so a client can echo them back in subsequent unsafe requests.
- Provide a protected write endpoint for posting comments and a public read endpoint for listing them. Storage may be in-memory (per-process).
- Provide a health endpoint that uses a safe method and is exempt from CSRF protection.

## Implementation Hints
- See the RedwoodSDK docs at https://docs.rwsdk.com for `defineApp`, `route`, global middleware, and interrupters. Middleware functions are placed at the top of the `defineApp` array; interrupters are placed before the final handler in a `route(path, [interrupter, handler])` array.
- A CSRF token of 32 hex characters can be produced from 16 bytes of `crypto.getRandomValues(new Uint8Array(16))`.
- The CSRF cookie and the submitted token must be compared as strings; reject the request when either is missing or they do not match. Use a constant-time comparison if possible.
- Cookies are read from the `Cookie` request header and set with `Set-Cookie` on the response. The framework's request object is a standard `Request`.
- Keep storage trivial (a module-level array). This is an evaluation app, not production.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Project path: /home/user/myproject
- Start command: npm run dev
- Port: 5173
- Token endpoint (always reachable, no CSRF check):
  - `GET /csrf` -> HTTP `200`, `Content-Type: application/json`.
    - Response body JSON shape: `{"token": string}` where `token` matches the regex `^[0-9a-f]{32}$` (32 lowercase hex characters).
    - Response MUST include a `Set-Cookie` header named `csrf` whose value equals the JSON `token` value, with attributes that include `SameSite=Strict` (case-insensitive) and `Path=/`.
    - Each request to `GET /csrf` MUST return a freshly generated token (two consecutive calls return different tokens).
- Health endpoint (always reachable, no CSRF check):
  - `GET /api/health` -> HTTP `200`, `Content-Type: application/json`, response body JSON `{"status": "ok"}`.
- Protected write endpoint:
  - `POST /api/comments` with `Content-Type: application/json`, body `{"author": string, "text": string}`:
    - When the request carries a `csrf` cookie AND an `X-CSRF-Token` request header whose value equals that cookie value -> HTTP `201`, `Content-Type: application/json`, response body JSON `{"id": number, "author": string, "text": string}` echoing back the submitted `author` and `text`. `id` MUST be a positive integer that is unique within the process.
    - When `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded` is used and the form body contains a `_csrf` field whose value equals the `csrf` cookie value -> the request MUST be accepted with the same `201` response shape (using the `author` and `text` form fields).
    - When the `X-CSRF-Token` header is missing AND no `_csrf` form field is present (with or without the cookie) -> HTTP `403`, `Content-Type: application/json`, response body JSON exactly `{"error": "invalid_csrf_token"}`. No new comment may be stored.
    - When the `X-CSRF-Token` header is present but its value does NOT match the `csrf` cookie value (or the cookie is missing) -> HTTP `403`, `Content-Type: application/json`, response body JSON exactly `{"error": "invalid_csrf_token"}`. No new comment may be stored.
- Public read endpoint:
  - `GET /api/comments` -> HTTP `200`, `Content-Type: application/json`, response body JSON array of comment objects in the shape `[{"id": number, "author": string, "text": string}, ...]`. The list MUST include every comment previously accepted by `POST /api/comments` in this process.
- General behavior:
  - The CSRF check MUST only apply to non-safe HTTP methods (POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH). Safe methods (GET, HEAD) MUST never be rejected because of CSRF.
  - The CSRF token MUST be generated dynamically per `GET /csrf` request; the implementation MUST NOT hardcode a constant token value.
task_integrations: - RedwoodSDK (rwsdk) - primary framework under evaluation; the app is run with `npm run dev` and tested via real HTTP requests.
truth:
# Verification Plan

## Setup
1. cd /home/user/myproject
2. Ensure dependencies are installed: `npm install` (already pre-installed in the Docker image; safe to re-run).
3. Start the dev server: `npm run dev` and wait for Vite to print `Local: http://localhost:5173/`. Allow up to 240 seconds for first-time startup (Vite + workerd cold start).

## Verification Steps

1. **Health endpoint is always reachable (no CSRF needed)**:
   - Request: `GET http://localhost:5173/api/health`.
   - Expected: Status `200`, `Content-Type` starts with `application/json`, response body JSON equals `{"status": "ok"}`.

2. **Token issuance endpoint returns a fresh hex token and sets a strict cookie**:
   - Request: `GET http://localhost:5173/csrf` using a fresh requests `Session` (call it `session_a`).
   - Expected:
     - Status `200`, `Content-Type` starts with `application/json`.
     - Response body JSON has a `token` field that matches the regex `^[0-9a-f]{32}$`.
     - The response includes a `Set-Cookie` header whose name is `csrf`. The cookie value equals the JSON `token` value. The raw `Set-Cookie` string contains `SameSite=Strict` (case-insensitive) and `Path=/`.
     - After this call, `session_a.cookies.get('csrf')` equals the JSON `token`.
   - Save the returned token as `token_a` for use in subsequent steps.

3. **Token is regenerated on each call**:
   - Using a SECOND fresh requests `Session` (call it `session_b`), issue another `GET http://localhost:5173/csrf`.
   - Expected: Status `200` and the returned token (`token_b`) is also 32 lowercase hex characters and `token_b != token_a`.

4. **POST /api/comments succeeds with valid header + cookie (JSON body)**:
   - Using `session_a` (which already carries the `csrf=token_a` cookie), send:
     - `POST http://localhost:5173/api/comments`
     - Header: `Content-Type: application/json`
     - Header: `X-CSRF-Token: <token_a>`
     - Body: `{"author": "alice", "text": "hello world"}`
   - Expected: Status `201`, response body JSON shape `{"id": <int>, "author": "alice", "text": "hello world"}`. Save the returned `id` as `id_alice`.

5. **POST /api/comments succeeds with valid `_csrf` form field (urlencoded body)**:
   - Using `session_a`, send:
     - `POST http://localhost:5173/api/comments`
     - Header: `Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded`
     - Body (urlencoded): `author=bob&text=second%20comment&_csrf=<token_a>`
   - Expected: Status `201`, response body JSON shape `{"id": <int>, "author": "bob", "text": "second comment"}`. Save the returned `id` as `id_bob`. `id_bob != id_alice`.

6. **POST /api/comments rejected when CSRF header is missing**:
   - Using `session_a` (cookie still present), send:
     - `POST http://localhost:5173/api/comments`
     - Header: `Content-Type: application/json`
     - NO `X-CSRF-Token` header, NO `_csrf` form field.
     - Body: `{"author": "mallory", "text": "no token"}`
   - Expected: Status `403`, response body JSON equals `{"error": "invalid_csrf_token"}`.

7. **POST /api/comments rejected when CSRF header does not match cookie**:
   - Using `session_a`, send:
     - `POST http://localhost:5173/api/comments`
     - Header: `Content-Type: application/json`
     - Header: `X-CSRF-Token: 00000000000000000000000000000000` (32 zeroes, clearly not equal to `token_a`)
     - Body: `{"author": "mallory", "text": "wrong token"}`
   - Expected: Status `403`, response body JSON equals `{"error": "invalid_csrf_token"}`.

8. **POST /api/comments rejected when there is no cookie at all (using a fresh session)**:
   - Using a THIRD fresh requests `Session` (`session_c`, no cookies set), send:
     - `POST http://localhost:5173/api/comments`
     - Header: `Content-Type: application/json`
     - Header: `X-CSRF-Token: deadbeefdeadbeefdeadbeefdeadbeef`
     - Body: `{"author": "mallory", "text": "no cookie"}`
   - Expected: Status `403`, response body JSON equals `{"error": "invalid_csrf_token"}`.

9. **GET /api/comments lists previously accepted comments**:
   - Request: `GET http://localhost:5173/api/comments` (no special headers required).
   - Expected: Status `200`, response body is a JSON array. The array contains an entry exactly equal to `{"id": id_alice, "author": "alice", "text": "hello world"}` and an entry exactly equal to `{"id": id_bob, "author": "bob", "text": "second comment"}`. None of the rejected attempts (`mallory`) appear anywhere in the array.

10. **GET methods are never rejected by CSRF**:
    - Using a FOURTH fresh requests `Session` with no cookies, send `GET http://localhost:5173/api/comments` and `GET http://localhost:5173/api/health` without any `X-CSRF-Token` header.
    - Expected: Both return status `200`.
